content.problemStatement:
You are given a chain of matrices
A1,A2,A3,…,An−1A_1, A_2, A_3, \ldots, A_{n-1}A1​,A2​,A3​,…,An−1​.

An array nums\texttt{nums}nums of size nnn. The dimension of matrix AiA_iAi​ (1≤i<n)(1 \le i < n)(1≤i<n) is nums[i−1]×nums[i]\texttt{nums}[i-1]\times\texttt{nums}[i]nums[i−1]×nums[i].

The task is to determine the minimum number of scalar multiplications required to multiply the entire chain of matrices by choosing the most optimal placement of parentheses.


Input Format

The first line of input contains a single integer TTT (1≤T≤103)(1 \le T \le 10^3)(1≤T≤103), denoting the number of test cases.
Each test case consists of two lines:

The first line contains a single integer nnn — the size of the array nums\texttt{nums}nums.
The second line contains nnn space-separated integers nums0,nums1,…,numsn−1\texttt{nums}_0, \texttt{nums}_1, \ldots, \texttt{nums}_{n-1}nums0​,nums1​,…,numsn−1​.




Output Format

For each test case, print a single integer — the minimum number of multiplications needed to multiply the given chain of matrices.
content.inputFormat: First line contains T, the number of test cases. Each test case contains two lines: the first line contains an integer n, the size of the array nums, and the second line contains n space-separated integers nums[0], nums[1], …, nums[n-1].
content.outputFormat: For each test case, print the minimum number of scalar multiplications required to multiply the entire chain of matrices.

sampleTestCases.explanation: Matrices: A1 10×15, A2 15×20, A3 20×25. Two possible parenthesizations give costs 11250 and 8000; the minimum is 8000.
Optimal order: (A1 × (A2 × A3)) × A4. Costs: A2×A3 = 4·6·2 = 48, then A1×result = 5·4·2 = 40, finally result×A4 = 5·2·7 = 70. Total = 48+40+70 = 158.
First test case has only one matrix, so cost is 0. Second test case is the classic example with dimensions 40×20,20×30,30×10,10×30; the optimal cost is 26000.

solution.approach: The problem can be solved using dynamic programming. We create a 2D array dp where dp[i][j] represents the minimum number of scalar multiplications required to multiply the matrices from i to j. We fill up the dp array in a bottom-up manner by considering all possible ways to split the chain of matrices and choosing the one that requires the minimum number of scalar multiplications.
solution.timeComplexity: O(T * n^3) per test case, O(T * n^3) total.
solution.spaceComplexity: O(n^2) — the dominant structure is the dp array.
